In this lesson, I'm going to give you an overview of the different follow-up sequence messaging and timing. Now throughout this module, we covered the eight different follow-up sequences, the eight different sequences that make up the follow-up for the entire six-figure funnel formula and the four-figure funnel formula. We talked about the prospect follow-up sequence going out to leads, individuals who opt in and don't buy. We talked about the main customer thank you sequence, which is for all new customers coming in designed to establish your credibility and expertise, as well as thank them for their purchase, motivate consumption, and drive to the webinar. We talked about the customer captivation sequence, or second chance for the reverse add-on. We talked about all the sequences for the different add-on offers, all the way down to the premium offer thank you sequence. Now let's talk about the different messaging, the themes of each of the emails in the sequences. What we're going to do is we're going to go sequence by sequence. I'm going to show you the number of emails that should be in each sequence, when those emails should go out, and the theme of each of the emails for each of the sequences. For the first sequence, which we're calling list zero, which is list zero in your Aweber, is the prospect follow-up sequence. Remember, the goal of this sequence is to drive prospects back to the customer captivation offer video to buy. The first email goes out immediately. The first email, the theme of that email is all about your hook and promise. It's taking your hook, your promise, that you've developed for your funnel, and it is reminding prospects about the hook, the promise, and driving them to go watch the video. They might have just opted into your traffic captivation page, and maybe they didn't get a chance to watch your customer captivation offer video. Maybe they left, maybe they accidentally closed their page. Regardless, this serves as an opportunity for them to watch the video if they didn't get a chance to watch the video, and or if they only watched a part of the video. This provides additional motivation for them to go back and watch the rest of the video. Day two, the very next day, this email is all about a customer transformation story. In an ideal world, this would be an existing customer who used your customer captivation product and went from where your average prospects are to where they want to be. This is an example of a story of a prospect of a customer that bought your product, was experiencing a certain problem, a situation, something that they wanted to overcome or something that they wanted to achieve, used your product, and achieved what they wanted to. Overcame the problem, relieved the issue that they were having, the same issue that your prospects want to relieve. In an ideal world, this is an existing customer. If you're just starting out and you don't have customers and therefore you don't have transformation stories that you can share, well, this can be a story of somebody that used the type of process or the type of system that you are teaching or giving or providing with your customer captivation offer. For example, if I was selling a product on how to use two-step marketing and I didn't have any customers right now, well, I could provide a transformation story about somebody that used two-step marketing and went from a poor functioning marketing process to a well-oiled machine. You can use real stories of individuals that you've heard about or that you've read about or that you've seen videos of and how they've went through a transformation. The final option that you have if you don't have existing customers is that you can use a fictional character. You're not presenting the story as truth. You're not presenting it as a customer. You're not lying, but you are presenting a story. You're telling a tale of an individual who was in the prospects shoes, made the decision to take advantage of your offer, and then experienced XYZ outcome. You're presenting it as a tale, as a fable, if you will. Let me tell you very quickly before moving on to the next email in this particular sequence that the best transformation stories are three phases. These are the three things, the three phases that you want to touch on in the story. Number one, you always want the main character in the story, the protagonist of the story, to be somebody that your prospects can relate to. In an ideal world, you want to have somebody who's similar to your prospects. If you're marketing to women, you don't want to have a man. If you're marketing to men, you really don't want to have a woman. You want it to be somebody, the character of the story, somebody that they can relate to. Next is, you want the start of the story to be where the main character is starting in a situation that's either the same as where prospects are right now or worse. Whatever handicap, whatever issue, whatever problem it is that your prospects are dealing with that your product or solution solves, that's where you want the prospect to start. If at all possible, you want the character of your story to start off in a worse situation. If you're a typical prospect, for example, if you're selling, get out of debt, training on getting out of debt, and your average customer or your average prospect is $10,000 in debt, well, maybe the main character in your story is $50,000 in debt and having their house foreclosed on. The reason why you want to start there is, number one, you want to show a story of somebody who started where the prospect is and ends up where the prospect wants to be all thanks to your product. The reason why you want to put the character in a worse situation or if it's true, you want to demonstrate that it's a worse situation because what that does is it triggers in the minds of prospects, oh, well, if this guy or if this woman can do it in a situation far worse than mine, I should easily be able to do it. The first part of the story is that it's a problem, the problem that your prospect is dealing with. The second part of the story is what we call the discovery. This is where the prospect discovered your program. Maybe that part of the story, which might just be a paragraph, is I looked high and low, I tried everything, I wasted money, and then I came across this product XYZ. It's this discovery. The same thing that your prospects are in the midst of right now, discovering your solution. The third and final part of the story is the transformation. What happened to this character after they made the discovery? What is their life like now? The picture of their life now should be the picture of the life that your prospects want to experience. This customer transformation story is showing prospects how they can go from where they are right now to where they want to be with your product without blatantly explicitly coming out and saying it, but by delivering it through a customer transformation story. That's day two. Day three is what we call problem agitate solve. This is where you bring up the problem again that your prospect is having. The whole reason why they came looking for your particular product or service or solution, your offer, the problem that they're having right now. We remind them of that problem and then we agitate the problem. We agitate the problem by showing them the outcome, the result of not doing anything about the problem or not using the right solution to solve their problem. If, for example, again they are in debt, we agitate the problem by talking to them about what happens the next time they go for a mortgage or for a car loan or for a credit card or for a business loan or anything like that. What can happen in terms of the credit card companies lowering their limit, canceling their card, whatever, right? Them not being able to get a job because their potential employer runs their credit report and so on. Then we solve the problem and we present again your product as the solution, the perfect solution to their current problem. We show how your offer, your solution, your product, your service, your software, whatever it is, is going to solve their problem better, faster, easier than any other option. Day number four is where we focus on a big benefit, really day four and then day five. The biggest benefit of your particular product or service or software or solution is what you want to present in this email on day four. You want to focus on the big benefit. That's the big benefit that prospects will reap when they buy your product. Then day five, you want to hammer home another big benefit. Day six, we follow up with a second customer transformation story. Again, using all the details that I already talked about from day two. Then finally, day seven is what we call the frankly un-puzzled and email. This is frankly un-puzzled over the last seven days ago. You requested access or you got access to a video and over the last seven days, I've shown you why. If you don't do anything about your problem, XYZ is going to happen. I showed you that when you do buy XYZ, you're going to get this benefit and this benefit. I've even shared with you a couple of stories of individuals that were where you are and now where you want to be and yet you still haven't ordered. It's got to be for one of the following three reasons. When you overcome the three common objections that prospects have for your particular product or service. If it's cost, you're going to show them how the cost is minimal compared to other options, other solutions. If there's no guarantee or if it's their concern that they're not going to get results, maybe you reconvey your guarantee. The point is it's frankly un-puzzled. I've shared all this information with you. You haven't bought. It's got to be for one of these three reasons. The three reasons that you cover are actually the three most common objections that prospects have to your offer and then you overcome those objections and you give one more link to go buy the customer captivation product. Now, list number two or list C is the main customer thank you sequence. Here's how this works. Day one is basically a welcome message and it's welcoming a new customer into your fold, into your tribe, letting them know that over the next handful of days, you'll be sending them a series of valuable emails and content and that you're going to take care of them, roll out the red carpet, et cetera. This is kind of, hey, welcome to the clan, welcome to the family now and I'm going to take care of you type of email. Day two and then day four and then day six. You could see it's day one is the welcome email. The very next day is what we call a consumption email. This is an email that drives them to consume your information product or to utilize your software or to utilize whatever it is that they've just bought. If it's information, you may be reminding them about a particular benefit that they'll reap or something cool that they're going to learn at a certain part in your information product. If it's a book, you might say be sure to pay attention to what's shared with you on page 36 because that does XYZ for you, big benefit, big benefit. Day two, then you skip a day. Day four, then you skip a day. Day six are all consumption based emails. These are all emails that are strategically designed or should be strategically designed to get customers to consume your information. Give them the reasons why they should be excited about the purchase that they made. Give them specific nuggets that they're going to learn and where in your product, where in your training, where in your information product they're going to get those things. Tease them. Tantalize them with what they're going to learn. Don't tell them in these emails what they're going to learn, but show them where in your product they're going to learn the things that they want to learn, that they need to learn in order to solve their particular problem. Then on day seven, you let them know about an extra unadvertised bonus webinar that they can go and register for. Then you include a link in this email to go send them to the bonus webinar. Day eight and day nine are two more reminders. Day seven, you announce the extra unadvertised bonus. Day eight, you remind them, if they haven't registered or you remind them about the webinar, day nine, you give them another friendly reminder. Then day 10 is when you host the actual webinar. You can certainly do this live. You can also have this evergreen, you can have this webinar be evergreen and just run on autopilot for you so that main customers are constantly going through this and individuals are exposed to this extra bonus webinar where at the end you make a premium offer on an ongoing, regular basis. That is list C, the main customer thank you sequence. Now we've got the customer captivation and second chance sequence. You're going to see that this sequence is very similar to the rest of the add-on sequences. These are really many sequences. Day one, like I've already explained to you, is where you basically say, here's how to access your customer captivation product. Here's how to access your XYZ product, your XYZ resource. Then day two, it's, hey, did you get a chance to access your customer captivation product, whatever the name of it is, whatever it is. Here is where you present the second chance offer for the first time in this sequence for the reverse add-on. It's, hey, I noticed that you didn't get a chance to pick up your copy of XYZ. Well, I want to give you a second opportunity to do that now that you've had the opportunity to see that I deliver the goods. You could go here to grab it. That link, of course, as you've learned already, takes somebody to the external reverse add-on page, like the reverse add-on on lead pages or whatever platform you use. Then day three, it's a reminder, hey, just want to remind you one more time that you still have an opportunity to grab the reverse add-on offer that you were first presented with right now. Go here. That is the customer captivation and second chance sequence. Then you'll see that the rest of the sequences, the rest of the add-on sequences are identical. In terms of these are many sequences that consist of just three emails. The first email giving them access to their product, the second email telling them about the second chance, and the third email reminding them about their chance. It's very cool because it's a very simple system. The soft add-on is a little bit different because the soft add-on starts day one giving them access to their training, their product, whatever it is. Now, because we are promoting with the mid-tier add-on offer, a higher priced offer, a mid-tier offer, we want to give them the opportunity to first consume the soft add-on. Now, remember, you heard me talk about that the soft add-on is a three-day or a 72-hour soft offer. They've got 72 hours to go through the soft offer or to consume the soft offer training or product or program before making a decision. We want to make sure that we drive consumption. Day two is a consumption email for the soft offer. Day three is a consumption email again for the soft offer training with a second chance offer for the mid-tier. Then, of course, on day four, a reminder about the second chance. Let me just again break this down. This is an individual that buys the soft offer add-on. Remember, the soft offer add-on is basically pay nothing now and only pay in 72 hours in three days if you love it. Because of that, we want to immediately give them access to the soft offer, to their training that they get with this, and then give them some consumption motivation, tease them, ventilate them, compel them to go through the soft offer training. Do the same the next day on day three because this is by the end of this day, if they've consumed it and like it, that's when they're going to get billed, that's when you're going to get the money. Here's where we want to present the second chance for the mid-tier. The next day, give them another second chance reminder about the mid-tier. That's list number three. List number four, of course, starts the same way. Your access to, you give them the access to their mid-tier purchase. Day two is about your purchase of the mid-tier and the second chance for the thank you page gift offer. Then day three is again a simple reminder about the second chance offer for the thank you page gift offer. List five, the thank you page gift offer sequence, number one, day one, gives them the access details to that offer. You know that I talked about that the thank you page gift offer is ideal for putting people into a continuity or membership program, a newsletter program or a membership site or something of that nature. In this email, you want to immediately give them their details to access their membership site and to get started. That's the first email and then the second email is basically just another like, hey, were you about to access your free gift or did you get a chance to access your free gift, et cetera. List six, day one is access to the premium product. Then of course, day two is consumption. Day three is consumption. Day four is a customer case study. This is not a transformation story. This is a customer case study. This is a case study to help motivate your customers to consume the content in this premium product. If this is a $600, $700, $800, $900 program, let's say it's an information product, well again, you're giving them access on day one. Then day two, you're giving them some motivation to consume, motivation to consume, again, teasing, tantalizing, all that good stuff. Now you're giving them an actual example of somebody that used the content and got great results. You're simply talking about the outcome that a customer got from this case study. You're talking about the benefits reaped, the results that the customer reaped. Then we skip a day and on day six, it's another consumption email. Then we skip a day and day eight is another customer case study. The whole purpose of this sequence, especially this portion of the sequence, is to drive consumption of the product. Remember that customers are only going to reap benefits from you when they actually consume your products. If it's an information product, for example, they're only going to reap benefit from you when they purchase it and consume it. Purchasing it alone is not going to get them benefits. We want them to get benefits. We want them to experience revelations, epiphanies, ah-has from your content because that makes them more likely to purchase and consume another product. If they buy a product and don't even consume it, the likelihood of them buying another product from you goes down significantly. That's what this sequence is all about.
